w:r.w:t: Here is the absolute, developer-ready technical blueprint for

This blueprint incorporates every single requirement you listed (Calendar generation, erratic moons, culture toggles, biome-linked resources, Voronoi map generation, image importing, and the instant auto-populator).
You can hand this directly to a C++ programmer (or an AI assistant) and say, "Build exactly this."
A high-performance, standalone C++ desktop application. It acts as the visual "God Engine," built to mirror the functionality of
. It does not run during gameplay. It is used strictly to design the physical universe, define the laws of physics/time, simulate history, and export a macro-level JSON state.
Dear ImGui (For all sidebars, sliders, and timeline logs)
jc_voronoi.h (Single-header library for Voronoi polygon generation) + FastNoiseLite.h (Fractal noise for natural terrain)
Based on your FantasyLloreAndWorldSimulator repository, structured for strict modularity.
├── deps/ # ImGui, FastNoiseLite, jc_voronoi, stb_image, nlohmann
│ ├── main.cpp # ImGui render loop and window lifecycle
│ │ ├── Viewport.cpp # Renders the Voronoi map visually
│ │ └── Panels.cpp # Settings, Timeline, and Entity editors
│ │ ├── VoronoiGen.cpp # Tectonic plates and polygon relaxation
│ │ ├── Climate.cpp # Wind vectors, temperature, and moisture
│ │ └── BrushTools.cpp # Manual editing of cell elevation/biome
│ │ ├── Calendar.cpp # Moons, Seasons, and Time math
│ │ └── EntityDB.cpp # Handles fetching Lore from Module 1
│ │ ├── AutoPopulate.cpp# Instant cities, A* roads, border walls
│ │ └── HistorySim.cpp # Faction expansion and warfare logic
│ ├── ImageImport.cpp # Translates PNG to Voronoi elevation
3. Core C++ Data Structures (The Internal Brain)
This is exactly how you must code the C++ structs to support your highly specific requirements.
// If true, cycle adjusts to fit evenly in a year
Scatter 20,000 points. Use jc_voronoi to build polygons.
Pick 10 random cells as plate centers. Expand outward. Where Plate A meets Plate B, push elevation to 1.0 (Mountains). Where they pull apart, drop to 0.0 (Ocean).
* Set Equator (Y = Center) temp to your user-defined max_temp. Set Poles (Y = 0, Y = Max) to min_temp.
Cast wind vectors across the map. If wind hits a mountain cell, add moisture to the windward side and subtract moisture on the leeward side (rain shadow).
Loop through all cells. Match the cell's final temp/moisture against the BiomeDef structs to assign the biome tag (e.g., "TUNDRA").
Instead of running a 1,000-year sim, the user clicks "Auto-Populate".
Find coastal or river cells with elevation < 0.4 and temperature > 0. Spawn a city. Assign it to a random Faction.
Pathfinding):** Run A* between cities. The "Cost" of moving from Cell A to Cell B is 1.0 + elevation_difference * 10. This forces roads through valleys and plains.
If Cell A is owned by Faction 1, and neighbor Cell B is Faction 2, mark the edge between them as "Border_Wall".
User selects a grayscale .png. Use stbi_load to read the pixel data.
Overlay the Voronoi grid on the image. For each cell, read the pixel brightness at its X/Y coordinate.
The app is a fullscreen ImGui window split into three main areas.
Input fields for "Era Name", "Year". ImGui::InputFloat for Moon cycles. ImGui::Checkbox for "Force Full Cycle".
Sliders for Min/Max Temp at poles/equator, Sea Level threshold.
On boot, sends HTTP GET to
to fetch all known plants, animals, and cultures.
Displays them in collapsible headers. You can click "Add to Map" or drag-and-drop a Faction onto a specific Voronoi cell.
Uses ImGui::GetWindowDrawList()->AddConvexPolyFilled() to physically draw the colored Voronoi cells.
Contains brush tools (Increase Elevation, Paint Biome, Place Settlement).
When you hit "Simulate 500 Years", a std::vector<std::string> fills with historical events:
"Year 42: Ice Nomads attacked Northern Horde (Loved resource dispute: Iron)"
When the user is done, they click "Export Game Data". It writes Saga_Master_World.json using nlohmann. Notice how it explicitly contains the modular data you requested, ready for the Python engine.
Ready to build the assembly line?
the heavy, global world-building logic you requested, but strictly outputs a clean JSON file so your Python game engine never has to calculate wind vectors or tectonic plates.
If this blueprint perfectly captures the "God Engine" you want to build, we are completely finished with
Are you ready for the detailed blueprint for
